retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin C is a hemoglobinopathy that is hereditary. It is an autosomal recessive disorder that results from the biparental inheritance of the allele that codes for Hemoglobin C. If both the parents are carriers of hemoglobin C, there is a 25% chance of having hemoglobin C disease in a child, 50% chance of having a child who is a carrier, and 25% chance of having a child who is neither a carrier nor have hemoglobin C disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin analysis reveals three different patterns of normal hemoglobin in an adult. These are Hb A (95 to 98%) containing two alpha and two beta chains, Hb A2 (2% to 3%) containing two alpha and two delta chains, and Hb F (less than 1%) containing two alpha and two gamma chains.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobinopathies are also relatively frequent AR disorders. Sickle cell disease and alpha and beta-thalassemia affect hemoglobin and its oxygen-carrying capacity.  In patients with sickle cell disease, the mutation causes hemoglobin to be a different shape that is not as conducive to carrying oxygen. These hemoglobin molecules polymerize into long chains that give the red blood cells their sickle shape. [9] These red blood cells do not have the same deformability as normal red blood cells and thus get stuck in small blood vessels. Affected individuals display characteristic traits such as splenomegaly, anemia, and swelling of limbs due to occluded blood vessels. In patients with thalassemia, the relative production of the alpha and beta-globin chains is altered, which impairs the assembly of the hemoglobin molecule; thus, there are not enough molecules to carry oxygen. [10] These individuals characteristically present with microcytic anemia, shortness of breath, hepatosplenomegaly. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The normal adult hemoglobin is a hetero-tetramer consisting of two pairs of globin polypeptide chains: one pair of alpha chains and the next pair of non-alpha chains. These polypeptide chains are folded such that the four heme groups lie in the clefts on the surface of the molecule forming the structure of the hemoglobin.

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin is made of iron (heme) and protein (globin). The function of hemoglobin is to carry oxygen from the lung to tissues. There are three main types of normal hemoglobin found in adults: Hemoglobin A, hemoglobin A2, and hemoglobin F are the types of globin molecule combinations, namely alpha, beta, gamma, or delta, determine the type of hemoglobin. All of the normal hemoglobin is a combination of alpha and non-alpha chains. The gene for alpha globin is located on chromosome 16. Hemoglobin A is composed of one pair of alpha-globin chains and one pair of beta-globin chains. It makes 95% to 98% of adult hemoglobin. Hemoglobin A2 is a pair of alpha chains and a pair of delta chains. It makes 1% to 3% of adult hemoglobin. Hemoglobin F is comprised of two alpha and two gamma chains. It makes up for the majority of neonatal hemoglobin, but in normal adults is 2% to 3% of the total hemoglobin. The percentages fluctuate based on age, genetics, medications, and underlying conditions. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several heterozygous combinations of hemoglobin C with other hemoglobin variants are seen. One case study reported a young woman with compound heterozygosity for Hb-D-Ibadan and HbC, a rare Hb combination that was previously not identified. This combination was detected during prenatal screening, and HbD-Ibadan was present in excess amount than HbC in the combination (70.3% to 24.4%). The combination is clinically silent and demonstrates thalassemia-minor-like red cell indices. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin (Hb) is a significant protein within the red blood cell (RBC). It comprises four globin chains, two derived from alpha-globin (locus on chromosome 16) and two from beta-globin (locus on chromosome 11). There are many subtypes of Hb. The most common ones that are found in adults without hemoglobinopathies are listed here: HbA1- comprises two chains of the alpha-globin and two chains of the beta-globin (a2b2) - This constitutes 95% of the adult hemoglobin. HbA2- comprises two chains of the alpha-globin and two chains of the delta-globin (a2d2) - This constitutes less than 4% of the adult hemoglobin. HbF- comprises two chains of the alpha-globin and two chains of the gamma-globin (a2g2) - This Hb is more prevalent in the fetus (due to the high oxygen binding affinity that helps extract oxygen from maternal circulation).

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The most common method used to detect hemoglobinopathies is hemoglobin electrophoresis or high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Patients who are homozygous for hemoglobin C disease mostly show HbC; HbA is absent, and HbF is slightly increased while patients who are heterozygous for hemoglobin C disease may show 30% to 40% HbC, 50% to 60% HbA, and HbA2 is slightly increased.

retrieved_docs.title: Thalassemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin electrophoresis: Hemoglobinopathy (Hb) evaluation assesses the type and relative amounts of hemoglobin present in red blood cells. Hemoglobin A (HbA), composed of both alpha and beta-globin chains, is the type of hemoglobin that typically makes up 95% to 98% of hemoglobin for adults. Hemoglobin A2 (HbA2) is normally 2% to 3% of hemoglobin, while hemoglobin F usually makes up less than 2% of hemoglobin in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: HbA, the most common adult form of hemoglobin, comprises two alpha and two beta-globin subunits. Inversely to HbF, HbA production explodes after birth and ultimately makes up 95-98% of hemoglobin in adults. HbA2 is a less common adult form of hemoglobin. It comprises two alpha and two delta-globin subunits and makes up 1 to 3% of hemoglobin in adults. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: There are a few different forms of normal hemoglobin in human blood. The percent prevalence of each hemoglobin type depends on the stage of development.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Thalassemia
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha-thalassemia comprises four subtypes based on the severity of the disease. They are all caused by alpha-globin gene deletions that negatively impact alpha-globin subunit synthesis. The difference in a subtype is the number of alpha-globin gene deletions. One gene deletion results in alpha-thalassemia (also known as alpha-thalassemia minima or a silent carrier), which has no significant hematologic consequences. Two gene deletions result in alpha-thalassemia (also known as alpha-thalassemia minor), which causes mild microcytic, hypochromic anemias. Two deletions on the same chromosome 16 ( cis deletion) are prevalent in Asian populations, whereas one deletion on each chromosome 16 ( trans deletion) is prevalent in African American populations. Three gene deletions result in Hemoglobin H (HbH) disease, which causes moderate to severe microcytic, hypochromic anemia, causing the accumulation of beta-globin subunits to combine and form beta tetramers (HbH). HbH is an unstable form of hemoglobin that precipitates and causes damage to erythrocytes as they age. Four gene deletions result in Hemoglobin Bart’s disease (Hb Bart’s), which is incompatible with life. The absence of alpha-globin subunits allows gamma-globin subunits in utero to combine and form gamma tetramers. Hb Bart’s has a high oxygen affinity and does not allow the release of oxygen to body tissues, leading to severe hypoxia of the infant and, ultimately, a lethal condition known as hydrops fetalis. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin C (Hb C), on the other hand, is one of the common structural variants of normal hemoglobin in which lysine is substituted for the glutamate in the sixth position of the beta-globin chain making it less soluble than Hb A. Hemoglobin C can either be in the homozygous states (Hb CC) or in the heterozygous states (Hb SC, Hb AC). Persons with hemoglobin C trait (Hb AC) are phenotypically normal and generally do not show any symptoms while persons with hemoglobin C disease (Hb CC) may present with mild chronic hemolysis, splenomegaly, and jaundice. Although hemoglobin C disease is a mild disease and does not develop into serious clinical complications, its inheritance with other hemoglobinopathies such as hemoglobin S (Hb SC) may have serious consequences. Thus, utmost care, treatment, and genetic counseling must be provided to these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fetal Hemoglobin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) is the dominant form of hemoglobin present in the fetus during gestation. HbF is produced by erythroid precursor cells from 10 to 12 weeks of pregnancy through the first 6 months of postnatal life. HbF contains 2 alpha and 2 gamma subunits, while the major form of adult hemoglobin, hemoglobin A (HbA), contains 2 alpha and 2 beta subunits. The genes that express gamma chain proteins are present in the beta chain locus on chromosome 11. The gamma subunit differs from its adult counterpart in that it contains either an alanine or a glycine at position 136, both of which are neutral, nonpolar amino acids. This difference introduces conformational changes to the protein, giving rise to several physiological differences in oxygen delivery that are important in fetal circulation. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thalassemias are a group of disorders caused by abnormal production of globin chains. The production can be diminished or can be absent for one or more of the globin chains. This imbalance of globin chain production impairs the production of normal hemoglobin. This impairment causes ineffective erythropoiesis with intramedullary hemolysis. Alpha thalassemia refers specifically to the abnormal or absent manufacturing of alpha-globin chains. These are associated with more than 15 different genetic mutations. The severity of the clinical condition is based on the mutation type. The severity of mutation is based on which of the two alpha-globin loci is affected. Mutations can also be deletion or non-deletion. In deletion mutation, there is an inheritance of a single alpha-globin gene. With the non-deletion type, a patient has inherited two alpha-globin genes, but one gene carries a non-deletion abnormality, for example, point mutation. In non-deletion, the severity of clinical expression is also affected depending on whether the mutation blocks the production of the remaining normal alpha chains partially or fully. Hemoglobin H disease occurs when only one normal alpha gene has been inherited. One of these most common non-deletion subtypes of Hemoglobin H is called Hemoglobin Constant Spring. HbH disease tends to be more severe in patients with the non-deletion-type likely due to interference with the transcription of the normal alpha chain gene by the abnormal one.

retrieved_docs.title: Beta Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thalassemias are a common cause of hypochromic microcytic anemia which arises from the reduced or absent synthesis of the globin chain of hemoglobin. Thalassemias are a quantitative defect of hemoglobin synthesis. This is in contrast with hemoglobinopathies, such as sickle cell disease, which are structural or qualitative defects of hemoglobin. Beta-thalassemia refers to an inherited mutation of the beta-globin gene, causing a reduced beta-globin chain of hemoglobin. The highest prevalence of beta-thalassemia mutations is in people of Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and Asian descent. Over 200 different thalassemia-causing mutations have been identified in the beta-globin gene, leading to the disease's wide genotypic and phenotypic variability. [1] The three classifications of beta-thalassemia are defined by their clinical and laboratory findings. Beta-thalassemia minor, also called carrier or trait, is the heterozygous state that is usually asymptomatic with mild anemia. Homozygosity or compound heterozygosity for beta-thalassemia mutations cause a more severe spectrum of anemias called beta-thalassemia intermedia and beta-thalassemia major. These two are distinguished clinically by transfusion dependence. Beta-thalassemia major requires routine transfusions, and intermedia does not.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) is clinically associated with thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Both disorders are hemoglobinopathies that can coexist in the same patient, and the presence of thalassemia may influence the ocular manifestations observed in sickle cell disease. Recognizing this association underscores the importance of comprehensive ophthalmic assessment and coordinated care for individuals affected by either or both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and dextran are both classified as members of the group “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions,” indicating that they serve as artificial blood products or plasma protein derivatives used to replace or augment the functions of red blood cells or plasma proteins in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 345
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is classified as a member of the broader category of blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions, indicating that it functions as a hemoglobin‑based oxygen carrier used to replace or augment blood components.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1568
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and hydroxyethylový škrob are both classified as members of the category “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions.” As blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions, these agents are designed to mimic or augment the physiological functions of blood components or plasma proteins, providing therapeutic options when conventional blood transfusion is unavailable or when specific protein supplementation is needed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions are classified as a type of blood and related products, and O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is identified as a member of the blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. This condition is clinically associated with قَرحَة (ulcer), reflecting the propensity for vaso‑occlusive events and tissue ischemia that can lead to ulcer formation. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (blood oxygen deficiency or hypoxemia), which results from impaired red‑cell flexibility and consequent reduced oxygen delivery. Recognizing that sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) can present with ulcers and low blood‑oxygen levels is important for comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.
